name: Arsenal
political_explanation: No notable, documented political identity attached to this club's supporters was found in available sources. The large majority of clubs across Europe fall into this category — most fan bases are not organised around a political identity, and it would be inaccurate to assign one without real evidence.

name: Manchester City
political_explanation: Historic identity as Manchester's working-class club, in contrast to Manchester United's larger, more nationally dispersed fanbase; a 2014 YouGov poll found City's support base distinctly left-leaning, and fan commentary continues to invoke this working-class identity even amid tension with the club's state-backed wealth.

name: Manchester United
political_explanation: No notable, documented political identity attached to this club's supporters was found in available sources. The large majority of clubs across Europe fall into this category — most fan bases are not organised around a political identity, and it would be inaccurate to assign one without real evidence.

name: Liverpool
political_explanation: One of the most extensively documented cases in football: manager Bill Shankly's explicit socialism shaped the club's self-identity; the fan-led Spirit of Shankly union campaigns on ownership, ticket prices and community welfare; supporters jointly run 'Fans Supporting Foodbanks' with Everton; and fans have long organised around Hillsborough justice, the Sun boycott, and solidarity with the 1995 Liverpool dockers' strike.

name: Chelsea
political_explanation: The Chelsea Headhunters firm has an extensively documented history of far-right and National Front/BNP association through the 1970s-90s, covered in academic hooliganism literature and journalism (e.g. Colin Ward's 'Steaming In', 'Headhunters'); describes a historic hooligan firm, not the current fanbase or club position.

name: Inter Milan
political_explanation: Historic identity as club of Milan's more conservative bourgeoisie; reporting has also linked far-right currents within hardcore ultras factions to nationalist politicians and neo-fascist-linked networks.

name: AC Milan
political_explanation: Historic identity as the club of Milan's railway workers and working class, traditionally contrasted with Inter's more conservative bourgeois support base — a divide widely documented in Italian football history (e.g. John Foot's 'Calcio').

name: Napoli
political_explanation: Ultras culture carries an explicit anti-establishment, anti-northern-racism political dimension tied to Southern Italian identity politics, with groups historically organising against discriminatory chants directed at Neapolitans by northern clubs' fans.

name: AS Roma
political_explanation: Curva Sud has a historic left-wing, anti-fascist tradition, traditionally positioned in contrast to city rival Lazio's far-right-associated Curva Nord.

name: Juventus
political_explanation: Ultras groups including the Drughi have a documented history of racist incidents (repeated monkey-chanting episodes) and reported links to right-wing regional politics, though the fanbase overall is not uniformly organised around a single ideology.
